requirement: Policies, processes, procedures and practices for mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks are in place, transparent, and implemented effectively.
competency_statement: Organizations must formalize and transparently implement comprehensive AI risk management policies and procedures, ensuring these are consistently applied and documented across the AI lifecycle [#21][#25].

requirement: Accountability structures empower appropriate teams and individuals for AI risk management.
competency_statement: A firm requires dedicated AI risk governance committees with cross-functional membership empowered to halt deployments when risk thresholds are breached [#21][#25].

requirement: Organizational culture considers and communicates AI risk.
competency_statement: Organizations must foster a culture where AI risks are openly discussed and communicated across all levels, supported by explicit risk governance policies [#21][#25].

requirement: Organizational teams are committed to a culture that considers AI risk.
competency_statement: Organizations should ensure that all teams are engaged in and committed to ongoing dialogue and education about AI risks, reinforced by governance frameworks [#21][#25].

requirement: Senior leadership and board members understand and contribute to AI risk management.
competency_statement: Senior executives and board members must be educated on AI risk and actively participate in oversight and strategic decision-making for AI deployments [#21][#25].

requirement: Policies address AI risks from third-party software, data, and supply chain.
competency_statement: Organizations must implement formal policies and controls to assess and mitigate AI risks arising from third-party software, data, and supply chain dependencies [#21][#25][#29].

requirement: Context established for AI system risks through understanding of intended purpose, impacts, and deployment context.
competency_statement: Organizations must implement lifecycle governance and orchestration patterns that ensure AI systems are contextualized for their intended purpose, deployment environment, and impact [#21][#09][#11].

requirement: Scientific findings and organizational practices integrated to characterize AI risks.
competency_statement: Organizations must operationalize mechanisms to integrate scientific evidence and organizational best practices into ongoing risk characterization and monitoring for AI systems [#21][#11].

requirement: AI risks and benefits mapped and prioritized by likelihood and magnitude of impact.
competency_statement: Organizations must deploy robust evaluation and monitoring infrastructure to systematically map, prioritize, and track AI risks and benefits by likelihood and impact throughout the system lifecycle [#21][#11].

requirement: Risks and benefits understood by the organization and relevant AI actors.
competency_statement: Organizations must implement monitoring and feedback mechanisms that ensure AI risks and benefits are communicated and understood by all relevant stakeholders [#21][#11].

requirement: Practices and personnel for supporting AI risk identification are in place.
competency_statement: Organizations must establish formal AI risk governance committees and structured processes for risk identification, including cross-functional teams empowered to proactively surface and escalate emerging AI risks [#21, #25].

requirement: AI risk measurement approaches are identified and applied.
competency_statement: Organizations must implement robust evaluation and monitoring infrastructure to systematically apply and track AI risk measurement methodologies throughout the system lifecycle [#21, #11].

requirement: AI system evaluated for trustworthy characteristics.
competency_statement: Organizations should deploy evaluation and monitoring systems capable of assessing AI systems for trustworthiness attributes such as reliability, safety, and fairness, including automated output validation where feasible [#21, #11].

requirement: AI system performance or assurance criteria are measured.
competency_statement: Organizations must establish continuous monitoring systems to measure and report on AI system performance and assurance metrics, ensuring alignment with defined benchmarks [#21, #11].

requirement: Feedback about efficacy of measurement incorporated into organizational processes.
competency_statement: Organizations should implement feedback mechanisms within their monitoring infrastructure to iteratively refine measurement processes and improve AI system performance over time [#21, #11].

requirement: A plan for managing AI risks is developed, monitored, and in place.
competency_statement: Organizations must formalize and maintain an AI risk management plan, assigning clear accountability and ensuring regular oversight and updates as part of governance processes [#21, #25].

requirement: Strategies to maximize AI benefits and minimize negative impacts are planned, implemented, and documented.
competency_statement: Organizations must document and operationalize strategies to maximize AI benefits and mitigate negative impacts, ensuring these are embedded in governance and compliance frameworks [#21, #25].

requirement: AI risks and benefits from third-party entities are managed.
competency_statement: Organizations must implement structured oversight and risk management processes for third-party AI vendors and supply chain partners, ensuring alignment with internal governance standards [#21, #25].

requirement: Risk treatments including response, recovery, and communication plans are put in place.
competency_statement: Organizations must develop and maintain incident response and recovery playbooks for AI risks, with clear escalation and communication protocols embedded in governance structures [#21, #25].

requirement: Policies address GenAI-specific risks: hallucination, data provenance, and synthetic content.
competency_statement: Organizations must establish and enforce policies that specifically address generative AI risks such as hallucination, data provenance, and synthetic content, ensuring these are reflected in both governance frameworks and technical controls [#22, #12, #13].

requirement: Agentic AI risks including prompt injection, tool misuse, and autonomous action boundaries are identified.
competency_statement: Organizations must establish formal risk identification processes for agentic AI, including mapping prompt injection, tool misuse, and autonomy boundaries, and ensure these risks are incorporated into enterprise AI risk policies and oversight structures [#21][#15][#29].

requirement: GenAI outputs evaluated for accuracy, bias, toxicity, and alignment with intended use.
competency_statement: Organizations need to implement robust evaluation and monitoring infrastructure capable of systematically measuring GenAI outputs for accuracy, bias, toxicity, and alignment with intended use, including automated output validation pipelines [#21][#11][#29].

requirement: Human oversight mechanisms in place for high-stakes agentic decisions.
competency_statement: Organizations must implement human-in-the-loop approval gates and override mechanisms to ensure that high-stakes agentic AI decisions are subject to effective human review and intervention before execution [#21][#15][#26].

requirement: High-risk AI systems must implement a risk management system throughout the lifecycle (Art. 9).
competency_statement: High-risk AI deployments require a comprehensive risk management system embedded across the AI lifecycle, supported by formal governance structures and cross-functional accountability for risk identification, mitigation, and documentation [#26][#21][#25].

requirement: High-risk AI systems must use high-quality data with appropriate data governance (Art. 10).
competency_statement: Organizations must establish and enforce comprehensive data governance policies, including data quality standards, provenance tracking, and access controls, to ensure that high-risk AI systems are trained and operated on high-quality, well-governed data [#26][#13][#12].

requirement: High-risk AI systems must maintain technical documentation sufficient to assess compliance (Art. 11).
competency_statement: Providers of high-risk AI systems must maintain comprehensive technical documentation that enables regulators and auditors to assess compliance with all applicable requirements throughout the system lifecycle [#26][#25].

requirement: High-risk AI systems must enable logging of events throughout operation (Art. 12).
competency_statement: High-risk AI systems must implement audit logging and telemetry infrastructure to record operational events, enabling traceability, incident investigation, and regulatory oversight [#26][#15].

requirement: High-risk AI systems must be transparent and provide information to deployers (Art. 13).
competency_statement: Providers must ensure that high-risk AI systems are transparent to deployers, supplying clear documentation and, where applicable, technical means to explain system behavior and data provenance [#26][#13].

requirement: High-risk AI systems must allow effective human oversight including ability to intervene and override (Art. 14).
competency_statement: High-risk AI systems must be engineered with effective human override and intervention mechanisms, ensuring that authorized personnel can monitor, intervene, and halt system operations when necessary [#26][#15].

requirement: High-risk AI systems must achieve appropriate accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity (Art. 15).
competency_statement: Organizations must implement technical controls for output validation, continuous monitoring, and robustness testing to ensure high-risk AI systems achieve and maintain appropriate levels of accuracy, reliability, and cybersecurity [#26][#29][#11].

requirement: GPAI model providers must maintain technical documentation and comply with copyright law (Art. 53).
competency_statement: Organisations must establish robust processes for maintaining comprehensive technical documentation and ensuring ongoing compliance with copyright law for GPAI models, supported by clear accountability structures and regulatory documentation practices [#26][#25].

requirement: GPAI models with systemic risk must perform adversarial testing and incident reporting (Art. 55).
competency_statement: Providers of GPAI models with systemic risk must implement adversarial testing frameworks, continuous monitoring infrastructure, and incident response protocols to detect, report, and mitigate emergent threats in line with regulatory expectations [#26][#15].

requirement: LLM01 Prompt Injection: Attackers manipulate LLMs via crafted inputs to cause unintended actions.
competency_statement: Enterprises must deploy robust prompt management systems and output validation mechanisms to detect and neutralize prompt injection attacks targeting LLMs, ensuring that crafted inputs cannot subvert intended model behavior [#29][#11].

requirement: LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure: LLMs may reveal confidential data in responses.
competency_statement: Organisations must implement fine-grained prompt management and data access controls to prevent LLMs from disclosing confidential or sensitive information in generated outputs [#29][#13].

requirement: LLM03 Supply Chain Vulnerabilities: LLM pipelines depend on third-party components with potential vulnerabilities.
competency_statement: Firms must establish and enforce supply chain risk management controls to assess, monitor, and mitigate vulnerabilities arising from third-party components in LLM pipelines [#29][#25].

requirement: LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning: Training data or model weights manipulated to introduce vulnerabilities.
competency_statement: Organisations must implement rigorous data governance and MLOps lifecycle controls to detect, prevent, and remediate data or model poisoning attempts throughout the LLM development and deployment process [#29][#11].

requirement: LLM05 Improper Output Handling: Insufficient validation of LLM outputs before passing to downstream systems.
competency_statement: Enterprises must deploy output validation and monitoring infrastructure to ensure that LLM-generated outputs are systematically checked for correctness and safety before being passed to downstream systems [#29][#11].

requirement: LLM06 Excessive Agency: LLM agents granted excessive permissions or autonomy beyond what is needed.
competency_statement: Organisations must enforce least-privilege access boundaries for LLM agents and implement human-in-the-loop approval gates to prevent agents from exceeding their intended scope of action [#29][#15].

requirement: LLM07 System Prompt Leakage: System prompts containing sensitive instructions are exposed.
competency_statement: Organisations must implement secure prompt management and secret handling protocols to prevent exposure of sensitive system prompts and instructions in LLM applications [#29][#11].

requirement: LLM08 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses: Vulnerabilities in vector stores and embedding pipelines.
competency_statement: Enterprises must architect secure RAG pipelines and enforce data governance controls to mitigate vulnerabilities in vector stores and embedding pipelines, ensuring data integrity and access security [#29][#13].

requirement: LLM09 Misinformation: LLMs produce factually incorrect content presented as authoritative.
competency_statement: Organizations must implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines and robust output validation mechanisms to ensure LLM responses are grounded in authoritative data and systematically monitored for factual accuracy, thereby reducing the risk of misinformation being presented as authoritative content [#13][#11][#29].

requirement: LLM10 Unbounded Consumption: LLM applications vulnerable to resource exhaustion and denial-of-service.
competency_statement: To prevent unbounded resource consumption and denial-of-service vulnerabilities in LLM applications, organizations must deploy real-time monitoring infrastructure and adopt scalable, modular architectural patterns that enable resource isolation and dynamic throttling [#11][#10][#29].
